Summary: Adjusted frontend auth UX so expired but refreshable sessions route into the MPIN unlock flow instead of logging the user out. Session refresh now distinguishes between transient refresh failures and hard refresh-token failures, preserving locked state for the former and forcing login only for invalid/expired refresh tokens.

Facts:
1. [finished] AuthSession in frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/auth_session.dart now tracks savedAt in memory, exposes isAccessTokenExpired, and schedules proactive refresh/retry behavior.
2. [finished] AuthSession.refreshIfNeeded now keeps a locked session on transient refresh errors but clears the session for hard auth failures such as invalid refresh tokens.
3. [finished] App startup in frontend/lib/app/app_startup_service.dart now routes expired authenticated sessions to AppRoutes.mpinUnlock when MPIN is enabled, instead of defaulting to login.
4. [finished] App resume handling in frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart now sends users to MPIN unlock when the session is expired, even if the normal background-lock timeout was not the trigger.
5. [finished] MpinUnlockScreen now refreshes the session before attempting MPIN verification so users with an expired access token can unlock without being forced through login first.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/auth_session.dart — Centralized refresh/session expiry detection and preserved expired-but-refreshable sessions so the app can route them into the MPIN lock flow. | Notes: Added savedAt tracking, isAccessTokenExpired, and refresh-failure handling that differentiates transient and hard failures.
2. [modified] frontend/lib/app/app_startup_service.dart — Startup route selection now sends expired authenticated sessions to MPIN unlock instead of login when MPIN is available. | Notes: This is the main entrypoint change for the new lock-state UX.
3. [modified] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart — App resume handling now checks for expired sessions and can push the MPIN unlock route directly. | Notes: This complements startup routing and keeps the UX consistent after backgrounding.
4. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/presentation/screens/mpin_unlock_screen.dart — The unlock screen now refreshes the session before MPIN verification, so an expired access token can be renewed as part of the unlock flow. | Notes: This keeps MPIN unlock functional after access-token expiry.
5. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/data/auth_local_store.dart — The persisted session model already gained expiry helpers needed by the refresh/lock flow and continues to back the new logic. | Notes: Existing helper coverage remains in the auth local store test file.
6. [created] frontend/test/features/auth/data/auth_local_store_test.dart — Regression coverage for session expiry helpers remains relevant to the new lock-before-login behavior. | Notes: The test still validates expiry timing logic.

content:
Summary: Improved frontend auth-session UX so access tokens refresh proactively before expiry and sessions are not dropped immediately on transient refresh failures. Also refreshed session state on app resume before MPIN/security checks, reducing surprise logouts while keeping auth boundaries intact.

Facts:
1. [finished] Auth session state is stored in frontend/lib/features/auth/data/auth_local_store.dart and now exposes expiry helpers for absolute expiry checks and proactive refresh windows.
2. [finished] AuthSession in frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/auth_session.dart now schedules refresh timers after login/restore and retries transient refresh failures instead of clearing the session immediately.
3. [finished] WAH4P app lifecycle handling in frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart now refreshes the session on resume before checking MPIN security state.
4. [finished] MPIN unlock in this codebase still requires a valid authenticated access token, so it is not an offline replacement for an expired auth session.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/data/auth_local_store.dart — Added explicit expiry helpers to the persisted session model so the auth layer can decide when to refresh before a token actually expires. | Notes: Added isExpiredAt and isExpiringSoon logic plus an expiresAt getter.
2. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/auth_session.dart — Centralized proactive session refresh, retry scheduling, and timer cleanup so token lifecycle is handled in one place. | Notes: Refresh is now scheduled after restore/login and transient failures are retried instead of hard-clearing immediately.
3. [modified] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart — App resume flow now refreshes the session before resolving MPIN/security state, reducing surprise expirations after backgrounding. | Notes: This keeps resume behavior aligned with the new auth-session lifecycle.
4. [created] frontend/test/features/auth/data/auth_local_store_test.dart — Added coverage for the new expiry and proactive-refresh window helpers to protect the auth-session timing logic. | Notes: Tests cover absolute expiry and the 5-minute refresh buffer.

Summary: Reviewed the name-field cleanup request and identified the exact frontend/backend boundaries. The app currently still exposes a separate second-name field in the registration and profile edit flows, while the backend profile DTOs and merge logic still accept and persist secondName. The received FHIR patient example with given=['Michael Christian','Aparicio'] should be interpreted in the UI as first name = Michael Christian and middle name = Aparicio, not as a second-name field. No SQL schema migration appears necessary because profiles already store given_names as a text array; the change is primarily field mapping and UI/API contract cleanup.

Facts:
1. [ongoing] The registration and personal information flows still render and submit a separate second-name field via shared name-form widgets and auth draft models.
2. [ongoing] Backend profile DTOs and merge logic still include secondName; current profile storage uses given_names text[] so no schema change is required for the requested rename/remap.
3. [ongoing] The FHIR patient example issue is a UI/contract mapping issue: given name element 0 should map to first name and element 1 should map to middle name in the profile edit UI.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/models/auth_api_models.dart — Defines the registration and profile draft contracts that currently include secondName and compose givenNames. | Notes: Needs a constructor and getter shape update.
2. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/auth/data/auth_api_client.dart — Serializes registration and profile update payloads, including the secondName field that must be removed. | Notes: Payload keys will need to match the simplified contract.
3. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/auth/presentation/screens/registration_personal_details_screen.dart — Registration screen owns the current name-entry controls and helper copy for the second-name field. | Notes: Entry screen needs copy and controller cleanup.

5. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/widgets/patient_profile_form_widget.dart — Profile edit form hydrates and saves secondName and middleName separately. | Notes: Hydration and save mapping need to collapse to first/middle/last.
6. [inspected] backend/src/auth/dto/profile-name.dto.ts — Base DTO still validates secondName and controls the API contract for registration/profile name updates. | Notes: Removing the property here propagates to both registration and profile DTOs.
7. [inspected] backend/src/auth/dto/update-profile.dto.ts — Manual profile update DTO still declares secondName explicitly. | Notes: Must be aligned with the simplified name contract.
8. [inspected] backend/src/auth/profile.service.ts — Profile merge logic currently builds given_names from firstName, secondName, and middleName. | Notes: This is the backend persistence boundary that must normalize to first/middle.
9. [inspected] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — FHIR incoming patient name parsing already extracts given name arrays; the user-visible bug is in downstream mapping rather than this extractor. | Notes: No change appears necessary for the basic given array extraction.

Summary: Implemented a backend fix so saving the Personal Information profile now mirrors PhilHealth and PhilSys identifiers into patient_identifiers. This resolves the receive-results patient matching failure caused by profile data living only in profiles.patient_profile. Facts:
1. [finished] Profile saves previously persisted philHealthId and philSysId only in profiles.patient_profile, not in patient_identifiers.
2. [finished] receive-results matches inbound bundles only through patient_identifiers system/value lookups, so missing rows there cause the 400 Bad Request match failure.
3. [finished] The new backend flow upserts canonical PhilHealth and PhilSys identifier rows into patient_identifiers whenever a profile is saved.
4. [finished] Targeted Jest tests for profile.service and profile.repository passed after the change.
5. [finished] A targeted eslint run still reported formatting and type warnings in the touched files after the functional fix.

Summary: Fixed the MPIN registration navigation crash by aligning the confirmation route type with the boolean result returned from the confirmation screen. Added a regression test that drives the MPIN setup flow through the app router and confirms it reaches the confirmation screen without a route cast failure.

Facts:
1. [finished] The MPIN setup flow in `frontend/lib/features/auth/presentation/screens/mpin_setup_screen.dart` pushes `AppRoutes.mpinConfirm` with `pushNamed<bool>`, so the router must provide a typed `Route<bool?>` for that path.
2. [finished] The registration crash was caused by `frontend/lib/app/app_router.dart` returning an untyped `PageRouteBuilder<dynamic>` for `AppRoutes.mpinConfirm`, which Flutter could not cast to `Route<bool?>` during `pushNamed`.
3. [finished] A regression test was added in `frontend/test/features/auth/presentation/screens/mpin_setup_screen_test.dart` to open MPIN setup through `buildAppRoute`, enter a 4-digit MPIN, tap Continue, and verify the confirmation screen appears.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/app/app_router.dart — Typed the MPIN confirmation route as `PageRouteBuilder<bool?>` so Navigator can cast the route result correctly during the registration flow. | Notes: This is the core fix for the crash.
2. [modified] frontend/test/features/auth/presentation/screens/mpin_setup_screen_test.dart — Added a regression test covering the setup-to-confirmation navigation path through the real app router. | Notes: The test fails if the route cast error returns.

Also added targeted unit tests for the new auth behavior and verified the backend build passes.

Facts:

4. [finished] The backend auth module now provides PasswordHistoryRepository and PasswordHistoryService alongside the existing auth services.

The app now mounts a global notification host from WAH4PApp, notifications slide down as neutral cards, and screens use AppNotificationCenter instead of ScaffoldMessenger snackbars. I also added a focused widget test for the new host and updated the sync-records screen test harness to include the notification host and dismiss the shared timer state.

Summary: Planned a repo-wide migration from ScaffoldMessenger snackbars to a reusable top-of-screen notification card in the Flutter frontend. Discovery showed about 57 snackbar callsites across 21 files, mostly in auth, PHR, profile, interoperability, and appointments screens plus one dialog. The proposed design is a shared notification banner component plus a global controller/service mounted at the app root so notifications can slide down from the top and work from any screen or dialog.

Facts:
1. [finished] The Flutter frontend currently uses many direct ScaffoldMessenger snackbars, with about 57 callsites across 21 files discovered in lib/features and one dialog widget.
2. [finished] The app root is WAH4PApp in lib/app/wah4p_app.dart, which already owns the MaterialApp and navigator key, making it the natural place to mount a global notification host.
3. [finished] The codebase already has shared UI patterns under lib/core/widgets/feature and lib/core/widgets/ui, so the notification card should live as a reusable shared widget instead of being duplicated per screen.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart — This is the MaterialApp entrypoint and likely host for the global notification system. | Notes: Contains the existing navigator key and theme setup.
2. [inspected] frontend/lib/core/constants/app_colors.dart — Needed to understand the current palette and avoid introducing conflicting notification colors. | Notes: Current primary color is purple, but the new notification should not rely on purple tint.

5. [inspected] frontend/lib/core/widgets/feature/app_bottom_sheet_widget.dart — Shows existing shared surface composition patterns for reusable feature-level widgets. | Notes: Useful styling reference for a new notification card/host.
6. [inspected] frontend/lib/core/widgets/ui/buttons/primary_button_widget.dart — Shows shared widget organization and styling conventions for reusable UI primitives. | Notes: Confirms the repo favors explicit reusable UI components.
7. [inspected] frontend/lib/core/widgets/ui/inputs/bottom_sheet_select_form_field.dart — Shows how shared widgets are structured and how overlays are used in the codebase. | Notes: Useful reference for building a reusable notification host or overlay behavior.

Summary: Implemented the next round of APC testing fixes for the auth/profile cluster. UAT-09 was clarified with explicit name guidance in registration and profile forms, the email verification resend cooldown was increased, MPIN confirmation now returns the user to setup when the code mismatches, and MPIN login now offers a recovery path into password reset. 4. [finished] frontend/lib/features/interoperability/domain/interoperability_models.dart now exposes the PH Core PhilHealth URI as the sync identifier system used by the Flutter app.

Summary: Fixed the backend webhook routing mismatch that caused /api/v1/fhir/receive-results to 404. The global prefix in backend/src/main.ts now applies cleanly to all routes without excluding the FHIR webhook endpoints, the webhook controller remains mounted at @Controller('fhir'), and the e2e route test now boots with the same api/v1 global prefix used in production. Verification passed for both build and FHIR e2e routing.

Facts:
1. [finished] backend/src/main.ts now sets the global prefix to api/v1 without excluding the FHIR webhook routes, so the webhook controller inherits the same public base path as the rest of the API.
2. [finished] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.controller.ts stays at @Controller('fhir'), which combines with the global prefix to produce /api/v1/fhir/... routes.
3. [finished] backend/test/fhir-sync.e2e-spec.ts now sets app.setGlobalPrefix('api/v1') so the e2e test matches production routing and verifies /api/v1/fhir/process-query, /receive-results, and /receive-push.
4. [finished] npm run build and the FHIR e2e test suite both passed after the routing fix.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/main.ts — Removed the FHIR-specific prefix exclusions so the global api/v1 prefix applies to the webhook routes. | Notes: This is the production routing fix.
2. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.controller.ts — Kept the webhook controller mounted under fhir so it combines with the global prefix to form /api/v1/fhir/... | Notes: This file was previously corrected away from api/fhir.

Summary: Relaxed personal information save behavior so the frontend and backend no longer require all fields for profile updates, while sync remains gated strictly by identifier presence. The backend now accepts partial profile drafts, merges them with existing profile data, and keeps the sync-lock enforcement introduced earlier. Frontend form fields were changed to optional validators and the sync button still depends on having at least one identifier. Regression tests were updated and targeted Flutter/Jest suites passed.

Facts:
1. [finished] Profile save/update now accepts partial drafts instead of requiring all personal information fields; missing values are preserved from existing profile data when present.
2. [finished] Sync readiness on the personal information screen is still identifier-gated only: the Sync records button remains disabled unless PhilHealth ID or PhilSys ID is present.
3. [finished] The profile form no longer marks fields as required in the UI and uses optional validators for all fields, matching the new save behavior.
4. [finished] Backend profile service now merges incoming partial profile drafts with existing profile data and still blocks edits when the profile has been sync-locked.
5. [finished] Frontend and backend regression tests were updated and the targeted Flutter and Jest test suites passed after the changes.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/auth/dto/update-profile.dto.ts — Converted the profile update contract to optional fields so saving changes no longer requires filling every personal information field. | Notes: All profile fields now validate only when non-empty.
2. [modified] backend/src/auth/profile.service.ts — Implemented partial-profile merge behavior and preserved sync-lock edit blocking. | Notes: Merges incoming drafts with existing row values instead of overwriting with empty strings.
3. [created] backend/src/auth/profile.service.spec.ts — Added regression coverage for partial save behavior and sync-locked update rejection. | Notes: New targeted unit tests for the profile service.
4. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/widgets/patient_profile_form_widget.dart — Removed required-field UI cues and switched the form to optional validation across all profile fields. | Notes: The widget now matches the relaxed save contract.
5. [modified] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen_test.dart — Adjusted expectations so the sync button remains disabled without identifiers and the form no longer asserts required markers. | Notes: Also updated button style assertions to WidgetState.

Summary: Implemented sync/profile lock workflow across backend and frontend: sync prepare now triggers a real gateway Patient request, Patient resource mapping now hydrates internal profile fields and marks profile as sync-locked, profile API exposes lock state, and personal information UI now enables sync based on identifier-only readiness and disables editing when sync-locked. Updated regression tests for profile readiness and sync navigation behavior, and validated with targeted Flutter and backend Jest suites.

Facts:
1. [finished] Backend integration prepare flow now calls the gateway /fhir/request/Patient endpoint after provider/identifier validation, so Prepare request triggers an actual Patient data request instead of preview-only behavior.
2. [finished] FHIR Patient mapping now hydrates internal profile schema fields (birthDate, gender, phoneNumber, identifiers, address fields) and sets a syncLocked marker in patient_profile to indicate gateway-synced profile lock state.
3. [finished] Profile responses now include isSyncLocked, and profile update attempts are blocked when profile is sync-locked, preventing manual edits after sync hydration.
4. [finished] Frontend profile sync readiness logic now gates only on identifier presence (PhilHealth ID or PhilSys ID), not full profile completion.
5. [finished] Personal information form now supports read-only mode; when AuthSession.profile.isSyncLocked is true, the form shows a lock notice, disables interactions, and hides save/reset actions.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.service.ts — Prepare sync now performs gateway Patient request and refactored gateway request helper supports POST payloads. | Notes: Added requestPatientSyncFromGateway and requestGatewayJson method split for GET/POST.
2. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.service.spec.ts — Test expectations updated because prepare sync now performs two gateway calls (provider fetch + patient request). | Notes: Added second mocked fetch response for gateway request endpoint.
3. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — Patient resource mapping now hydrates internal profile fields and marks profile as syncLocked. | Notes: Added identifier/address/telecom extraction helpers and lock marker assignment.
4. [modified] backend/src/auth/auth.types.ts — Profile API contract now includes isSyncLocked state for frontend read-only handling. | Notes: Extended PatientProfileResponse interface.
5. [modified] backend/src/auth/profile.repository.ts — Profile response generation now computes isSyncLocked and exposes helper for lock-state detection. | Notes: Added isPatientProfileSyncLocked utility.
6. [modified] backend/src/auth/profile.service.ts — Manual profile updates are now blocked when profile is sync-locked. | Notes: Added BadRequestException guard before upsert.
7. [modified] backend/src/auth/login-auth.service.ts — Default empty profile response now includes isSyncLocked for contract consistency. | Notes: Updated EMPTY_PATIENT_PROFILE_RESPONSE.
8. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/models/auth_api_models.dart — Frontend profile model now reads/writes isSyncLocked from API responses. | Notes: Extended UserProfileSummary constructor, fromJson, empty, and toJson.
9. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/domain/profile_sync_readiness.dart — Sync readiness now depends only on identifier presence as requested. | Notes: Removed completeness requirement from readiness state.
10. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen.dart — Personal information screen now passes read-only state to form and uses identifier-centric sync guidance copy. | Notes: Uses AuthSession.profile.isSyncLocked for form locking.
11. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/widgets/patient_profile_form_widget.dart — Profile form now supports read-only mode for synced/locked profiles. | Notes: Wrapped content with IgnorePointer/Opacity and hides action buttons when read-only.

13. [modified] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen_test.dart — Updated fake interoperability client and expectations for revised sync readiness guidance. | Notes: Added simulateSyncRequest stub and removed outdated required-profile-details expectation.
14. [modified] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/widgets/patient_profile_form_widget_test.dart — Updated UserProfileSummary fixture with new isSyncLocked field. | Notes: Constructor compatibility update.

Summary: Fixed the sync simulation failure that surfaced as 'unable to store observation record' by making the backend observation/lab record filter text length-safe before insert. The root cause was an overlong laboratory_result_records.filter_value exceeding the database's 80-character check constraint. Added a reusable filter builder in the FHIR sync mapper so clinical record filter values are bounded, and added regression coverage for the PH Core parsers to assert filterValue length stays within 80 characters. Verified with targeted backend tests, backend build, and the sync simulation e2e route test.

Facts:
1. [finished] The sync simulation flow now authenticates with the Supabase access token and stores simulated results against the authenticated account context rather than a user-facing profileId.
2. [finished] The observation storage failure was caused by an overlong filter_value for laboratory_result_records, which violated the database check constraint of 80 characters.
3. [finished] A reusable length-safe filter builder was added to backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts and used for clinical record filter values.
4. [finished] A regression test was added in backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.registry.spec.ts to assert clinical filterValue strings remain within 80 characters.
5. [finished] Verification completed successfully with npm test for the FHIR sync parser/service specs, npm run build, and npm run test:e2e -- --runInBand app.e2e-spec.ts in backend/.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — Added the length-safe filter builder and updated clinical record mapping so inserted filter values cannot exceed the database constraint. | Notes: This is the primary production fix for the observation storage failure.
2. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.registry.spec.ts — Added regression coverage asserting clinical record filter values stay within the 80-character database limit. | Notes: Covers the observation fixture path that previously failed.
3. [inspected] backend/package.json — Confirmed the available backend test and build scripts before running validation. | Notes: No file changes were needed.
4. [inspected] sqls/007_health_records_setup.sql — Confirmed the laboratory_result_records schema and its 80-character filter_value constraint to identify the root cause. | Notes: Read-only inspection only.
5. [inspected] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.service.ts — Verified the persistence flow that inserts parsed clinical resources into the repository. | Notes: No code change required in this file.

Summary: Fixed the sync simulation auth-context problem by switching the frontend to send the authenticated Supabase access token as a Bearer Authorization header, and by making the backend simulation route resolve the current user from that token through AuthSupportService, with x-user-id only as a fallback. The request body no longer carries profileId, and the simulation still persists the PH Core bundle against the authenticated account via the existing receive-results-for-profile path. Validation passed after the change.

Facts:
1. [finished] The simulation flow now authenticates with Authorization: Bearer <accessToken> from AuthSession.accessToken instead of a request-body profileId.
2. [finished] The backend simulation route resolves the current account through AuthSupportService.getAuthenticatedUserFromHeader when an Authorization header is present, with x-user-id retained only as a fallback.
3. [finished] The user-facing simulation request body now contains only provider and identifier fields; profileId is no longer exposed to the UI or request payload.
4. [finished] The task card 'Remove profileId from sync simulation request' was moved to done after the bearer-token fix and verification.

Affected files:

4. [modified] backend/src/integration/sync-simulation.service.ts — Resolved profile ID from the bearer token using AuthSupportService, with x-user-id fallback, before persisting the simulated PH Core bundle. | Notes: This is the core runtime fix for the missing account context error.
5. [modified] backend/src/integration/dto/simulate-sync-request.dto.ts — Removed profileId from the public simulation DTO so the body only contains request data relevant to the sync action. | Notes: Prevents users from having to provide internal UUIDs.
6. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.types.ts — Removed the obsolete public simulation payload type that exposed profileId. | Notes: Keeps the contract aligned with the new auth-based flow.

Summary: Adjusted the sync simulation flow so the user-facing request no longer carries a profileId in the body. The frontend now sends the authenticated account ID in an internal x-user-id header, while the backend simulation endpoint resolves the profile context from that header and persists the generated PH Core bundle through the existing receive-results-for-profile path. This preserves UUID-based internal storage without exposing profileId to the user and resolved the validation error the user was seeing.

Facts:
1. [finished] The simulation endpoint no longer accepts a user-facing profileId in the JSON body; it now reads the authenticated account context from the x-user-id header.
2. [finished] The Flutter interoperability client and sync wizard now send x-user-id from AuthSession.userId when calling the simulation endpoint, while keeping the body limited to provider and identifier fields.
3. [finished] The backend SyncSimulationService still builds a PH Core Bundle from checked-in example fixtures, but now persists it via receiveResultsForProfile using the header-derived account ID.
4. [finished] The task card 'Remove profileId from sync simulation request' was created and moved to done after the fix and verification.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/integration/dto/simulate-sync-request.dto.ts — Removed profileId from the public simulation request DTO so the request body no longer requires a UUID field from the user. | Notes: This is the main API-shape fix.
2. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.controller.ts — Changed the simulation route to read the internal x-user-id header and pass it to the simulation service. | Notes: Keeps account context internal to the transport layer.
3. [modified] backend/src/integration/sync-simulation.service.ts — Updated the simulation service to require a header-derived user ID and persist the simulated bundle against that profile context. | Notes: No longer depends on a body-level profileId.
4. [modified] frontend/lib/features/interoperability/data/interoperability_api_client.dart — Changed the simulation client to send x-user-id as a header instead of profileId in the body, and added header merging support to the POST helper. | Notes: The body now matches the user-facing request shape.
5. [modified] frontend/lib/features/interoperability/presentation/screens/sync_records_wizard_screen.dart — Updated the wizard to pass the signed-in session user ID into the simulation client and keep the sign-in guard for missing context. | Notes: The button stays hidden from UUID details.
6. [modified] backend/test/app.e2e-spec.ts — Updated the e2e simulation request to send x-user-id and removed the profileId body field. | Notes: Verifies the new transport shape.

The backend now exposes a simulated sync endpoint that reuses the existing prepare flow for validation, loads checked-in PH Core fixtures, and forwards a Bundle into the existing FHIR receive-results persistence path. Summary: Implemented a strict PH Core-aware FHIR inbound parsing layer for the backend webhook flow. The service now validates inbound receive-results and receive-push payloads through per-resource parser modules before mapping and persisting data, and the shared mapper was improved to handle array-shaped encounter code fields from PH Core examples. Added fixture-driven parser and service tests, and verified the backend e2e suite still passes.

Facts:
1. [finished] Added PH Core-aware parser modules under backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers for patient, immunization, encounter, observation, condition, procedure, and medication request payloads.
2. [finished] Parser validation now requires a matching PH Core profile suffix for supported resource types before payloads are accepted.
3. [finished] The FhirSyncService now routes receive-results and receive-push payloads through parseInboundResource before Supabase persistence.
4. [finished] The shared FHIR mapper was updated so encounter codeable-concept text can be read from array-shaped PH Core fields.
5. [finished] New parser/service specs use PH Core fixture JSON files from resources/examples/ph-core and the test suite passes.
6. [finished] Backend e2e tests still pass after the parser/service refactor.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.service.ts — Wired inbound webhook handling through the new parser registry before persistence, keeping route handling thin and validating PH Core payloads first. | Notes: receiveResults and receivePush now parse resources before repository writes.

Summary: Implemented the first-pass inbound WAH4PC/FHIR sync flow for wah4patients. The backend now has a dedicated fhir-sync module for provider-facing webhooks and practitioner discovery, routes are excluded from the global /api/v1 prefix where needed, patient identifier matching is backed by a new Supabase table, and inbound resources are normalized into the existing internal clinical tables instead of storing raw payloads. Validation and tests passed after the changes.

Facts:
1. [finished] The backend now includes a new fhir-sync module with provider webhook controllers, a gateway auth guard, a sync service, a sync repository, and mapper/types files under backend/src/fhir-sync.

3. [finished] The backend already had internal tables for immunization_records, medical_history_records, medical_consultation_records, and medication_resupply_history_records, and the new flow writes normalized records into those tables instead of persisting raw FHIR JSON.
4. [finished] Gateway-facing webhook routes are exposed as /api/fhir/practitioners, /fhir/process-query, /fhir/receive-results, and /fhir/receive-push, with the global api/v1 prefix excluded for those paths.
5. [finished] Environment validation now requires WAH4PC_GATEWAY_AUTH_KEY and includes WAH4PC_PRACTITIONER_LIST_ENDPOINT for the provider-side inbound flow.

Summary: Implemented clickable dashboard weekly metric cards and improved BMI startup behavior so the screen no longer flashes the add form before determining whether today's BMI already exists. DashboardMetricCard is now tappable via an optional callback, and DashboardScreen maps BMI, blood pressure, and temperature cards to their existing AppRoutes. Summary: Implemented Phase 2 of the interoperability rollout across backend and Flutter. The backend now exposes a WAH4PC-backed interoperability proxy for provider discovery and sync-request preparation, with env validation and e2e coverage. The Flutter app now launches a dedicated sync wizard from the Personal Information screen, uses bottom-sheet selection for identifier/provider choice, and reuses the existing profile readiness rules to gate entry. Tests for the new interoperability models, wizard flow, and profile-to-wizard navigation all pass.

1. [finished] The backend interoperability proxy now exposes GET /api/v1/interoperability/providers and POST /api/v1/interoperability/sync/prepare, backed by the real WAH4PC gateway endpoint wah4pc.echosphere.cfd.

4. [finished] The Flutter wizard uses existing bottom-sheet selection controls to pick a PhilHealth or PhilSys identifier and then an active provider, while preserving profile readiness gating from Phase 1.

No create/receive endpoints were added, matching the user's request to handle creation later. Validation passed with backend npm run build and frontend flutter analyze.

3. [finished] Flutter Health Records screens now use HealthRecordDataScreen to load entries through HealthRecordsApiClient with the existing bearer token from AuthSession.
4. [finished] The HealthRecordScreenTemplate remains presentation-focused for search, filtering, expansion, help, and empty states, while data loading and mapping are separate responsibilities.

Summary: Implemented read-only Supabase-backed Health Records storage integration. Added SQL schema for a single health_records table designed for manual Supabase inserts and future backend create endpoints, added backend database typings and a read-only NestJS HealthRecords module, and refactored Flutter Health Records screens to load authenticated records from the backend without adding any create/receive endpoints. Validation passed with backend npm run build and frontend flutter analyze.

Facts:
1. [finished] Health Records now use a new Supabase table named public.health_records with section_key, title/subtitle, summary fields, filter/status fields, status/accent tone keys, icon_key, details_json, display_order, recorded_at, created_at, and updated_at.
2. [finished] The backend exposes a read-only GET /health-records?section=<section_key> endpoint protected by the existing API key guard and bearer access-token user resolution; no POST/create endpoint was added.
3. [finished] The Flutter Health Records screens no longer contain hardcoded HealthRecordEntry sample data; they use a shared HealthRecordSectionScreen that loads records by section and maps API responses into the existing HealthRecordScreenTemplate.
4. [finished] Manual Supabase inserts should use section_key values medical_history, immunization_records, medical_consultations, or laboratory_results and details_json as an array of objects with label and value string fields.

Affected files:

content:
Summary: Reviewed the frontend Health Records screens and the existing Personal Records architecture to plan a future local persistence layer for Health Records. The current Health Records feature is still presentation-driven with hardcoded entry data in the screen files, while Personal Records already has a dedicated typed data/repository pattern. The recommended approach is to introduce a separate local health-records repository and domain model layer so the UI can read from a single source of truth now and later support creation without reworking the screens.

Facts:
1. [finished] The frontend Health Records feature currently stores its record entries directly inside the screen files, especially the Medical History, Immunization Records, Medical Consultations, and Laboratory Results screens.
2. [finished] The Health Record screen template already supports filtering, search, and expansion, so it can stay as the presentation layer while data sourcing moves behind a repository.
3. [finished] The Personal Records feature already uses a dedicated typed repository/client pattern, which is the best internal reference for organizing a future local Health Records persistence layer.

Affected files:

4. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/widgets/health_record_screen_template.dart — This widget owns search, filtering, and expansion behavior and should stay separate from storage concerns. | Notes: It is the primary screen composition layer.
5. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/medical_history_screen.dart — This screen currently embeds hardcoded health record entries and is a primary candidate for repository-backed data. | Notes: Reviewed its static record list.

9. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/phr/data/personal_records_api_client.dart — This typed API client provides the strongest existing precedent for how record data should be modeled and loaded in a dedicated layer. | Notes: Used as the architecture comparison point.

Summary: Implemented the first Personal Records persistence slice with separate Supabase tables for BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medicine intake. Added a new SQL migration, updated typed database contracts, and aligned the schema documentation and backend README with the new tables. Verified the backend still builds successfully after the type updates.

Facts:
1. [finished] A new Supabase migration file was added for Personal Records: BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medication intake now each have their own table with timestamps, ownership, indexes, and RLS policies.
2. [finished] The backend Supabase database types were expanded to include bmi_records, blood_pressure_records, temperature_records, and medication_intake_records with explicit Insert/Update/Row contracts.
3. [finished] The schema documentation in data-structure.md was updated so the Personal Records section now points to the new separate tables instead of a generic observations-only approach.
4. [finished] backend/README.md now references both the original auth/profile bootstrap script and the new personal-records bootstrap script.
5. [finished] BMI records now include a measurement_system field so the stored record preserves whether the user entered metric or imperial input.

Affected files:

content:
Summary: Added a plain-English atlas for WAH4P so future work can read the project like a map. The repo is best understood in layers: a Flutter client that controls navigation and presentation, a NestJS backend that owns security and business rules, and Supabase/Postgres plus secure local storage for persistence. The most important user journeys now have an explicit flow: splash/startup -> onboarding or login -> dashboard or MPIN unlock -> feature areas like records, appointments, resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. This memory also clarifies which screens are static, which are route-driven, and which backend APIs support each major journey.

Facts:
1. [finished] Think of the repository as three layers: frontend/ is the Flutter presentation and navigation layer, backend/ is the NestJS security and business-logic layer, and sqls/ plus the data-structure docs define the Supabase/Postgres persistence layer.
2. [finished] The app begins at the Flutter shell in frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart, which owns theme setup, route generation, startup gating, and resume-time MPIN locking; the visible launch flow is controlled from there rather than from individual screens.
3. [finished] Startup behavior is intentionally simple to follow: the app checks whether a valid session exists, whether onboarding is complete, and whether MPIN unlock is required, then routes the user to onboarding, login, dashboard, or the MPIN unlock screen.
4. [finished] The Flutter route system is centralized in frontend/lib/app/app_routes.dart and frontend/lib/app/app_router.dart, so route names and screen mappings should be edited there first when changing navigation.
5. [finished] The route map forms a clear product atlas: splash and onboarding lead into registration and login, then into the dashboard, and from there into health records, personal records, appointments, medication resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, security settings, and legal/privacy screens.
6. [finished] The frontend feature folders mirror product domains, with separate areas for auth, onboarding, dashboard, health_records, phr, appointments, calendar, medication_resupply, profile, notification, legal, and splash.
7. [finished] The auth feature is one of the largest frontend domains and includes the registration screens, login, email verification, forgot password, MPIN setup/unlock, TOTP challenge/setup, and security verification flows.
8. [finished] The backend app module in backend/src/app.module.ts wires configuration, throttling, the health module, and the auth module, while global guards enforce API-key access and rate limiting across the server.
9. [finished] The backend health endpoint is intentionally public and small, while the rest of the server is protected unless a route is marked public with the custom decorator.
10. [finished] The backend auth module is the server-side workflow hub and splits responsibilities into registration, login, password reset, profile, TOTP, MPIN, and support services/repositories instead of one monolithic auth implementation.
11. [finished] The backend auth controller groups the API by user journey: registration OTP request/verify/complete, password reset request/verify/complete, login and refresh, TOTP setup and verification, security verification, MPIN setup/verification/device registration, and device unregister flow.
12. [finished] The backend profile controller is separate from the login flow and handles authenticated profile retrieval and updates via /profile/me, which matches the frontend profile and personal information screens.
13. [finished] The project’s data rules are to keep canonical patient data in Supabase/Postgres, keep session/onboarding/device flags in secure local storage, and treat dashboard summaries and similar UI aggregates as derived data rather than separate source records.
14. [finished] Some documentation files are secondary references rather than the live truth, especially the older screen inventory; for navigation work, the live Flutter route constants and route builder are the authoritative map.

Summary: Saved a deeper project map for WAH4P that focuses on navigation, feature boundaries, and service responsibilities. The repository presents a clear two-tier architecture: a NestJS backend that owns auth/security/business rules and a Flutter frontend that owns display, navigation, and device-scoped state. The frontend route tree now has a precise map from startup through onboarding, auth, dashboard, PHR, EHR-style records, appointments, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. The backend API surface is also clearer: a public health check plus a broad auth/profile domain that covers registration, login, 2FA, MPIN, password reset, and profile hydration/update. This memory layer is intended to function as an app map, not just a summary.

Facts:
1. [finished] The Flutter client has a single centralized app shell in frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart, and that shell owns the Material theme, navigator key, route generation, startup route wiring, and resume-time MPIN lock behavior.
2. [finished] The app startup path is explicit: frontend/lib/app/app_startup_service.dart resolves the first route based on refreshed auth session, onboarding completion, and MPIN device state, returning onboarding, login, dashboard, or MPIN unlock.
3. [finished] The startup gate screen in frontend/lib/app/startup_gate_screen.dart is only a transient decision screen; it shows loading/status text, then immediately replaces itself with the resolved initial route.
4. [finished] The app lock policy is device-state driven: frontend/lib/app/app_lock_state_service.dart marks the app backgrounded and requires unlock after a five-minute timeout on resume when the user is authenticated.
5. [finished] The route registry is centralized in frontend/lib/app/app_routes.dart, and the route builder in frontend/lib/app/app_router.dart maps every visible screen to a named route with mostly fade, slide, or instant transitions.
6. [finished] The Flutter route map covers these major user journey groups: splash/onboarding, registration, login and MFA, dashboard, health records, personal records, appointments, medication resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, security settings, and legal/privacy.
7. [finished] The frontend feature tree is organized by responsibility under frontend/lib/features/, with dedicated folders for auth, dashboard, health_records, medication_resupply, phr, profile, onboarding, splash, calendar, notification, legal, and appointments.
8. [finished] The backend application module in backend/src/app.module.ts wires configuration, throttling, the auth module, and the health module, while also installing global guards for API-key authorization and rate limiting.
9. [finished] The backend public surface is intentionally small: backend/src/health/health.controller.ts exposes a public GET /health endpoint, while all other routes are protected by the API-key guard unless explicitly marked public.
10. [finished] The backend auth module in backend/src/auth/auth.module.ts is the main domain module and composes controllers plus specialized services for registration, login, password reset, TOTP, MPIN, profile, and supporting repositories.
11. [finished] The backend auth controller groups the API into clear workflow families: registration OTP flow, password reset flow, login and refresh, TOTP setup/challenge, security verification, MPIN set/verify/register/unregister, and device/profile-related auth support.
12. [finished] The backend profile controller in backend/src/auth/profile.controller.ts is separate from the auth workflow endpoints and provides /profile/me GET and PATCH handlers for authenticated profile hydration and updates.
13. [finished] The project docs still matter as design references: data-structure.md defines Supabase/Postgres as the system of record and local secure storage for session/onboarding/device flags, while checklist.md tracks unfinished persistence and validation work.
14. [finished] The current source tree and the older screen inventory docs are not perfectly aligned, so the live Flutter route map should be treated as the authoritative reference for actual navigation until the docs are reconciled.

Summary: Audited the WAH4P repository and saved a clear project snapshot for future work. The repo is split into a NestJS backend, a Flutter frontend, and supporting SQL/docs. The backend is a secured API with global API-key protection, throttling, strict env validation, Supabase auth/data integration, and Resend email delivery. The frontend is a Flutter mobile app with centralized theming, secure local storage, a startup gate, and MPIN/session lifecycle handling. The docs describe a Supabase/Postgres source of truth for patient data, with frontend screens mostly acting as presentation and interaction layers. I also captured the current testing surface and a couple of documentation mismatches that future work should reconcile.

Facts:
1. [finished] The repository is organized as a multi-part WAH4P project with backend/, frontend/, sqls/, and documentation files at the root, so future changes should treat it as a full-stack product rather than a single app.
2. [finished] The backend is a NestJS API under backend/ that uses Supabase for auth/data, Resend for email OTP delivery, global API-key protection, throttling, and strict Joi-based environment validation.
3. [finished] The backend HTTP entrypoint sets the global API prefix to /api/v1, enables helmet, applies a whitelist/transform ValidationPipe, and reads CORS origins from FRONTEND_ORIGIN.
4. [finished] The frontend is a Flutter app under frontend/ with a centralized Material theme, Open Sans typography, secure storage, and a startup gate flow driven by app state and route generation.
5. [finished] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart manages app lifecycle behavior, including MPIN lock handling on resume by checking local device state and backend security status before showing the unlock route.
6. [finished] Project docs in data-structure.md and checklist.md define Supabase/Postgres as the canonical source of truth for patient data, while local storage is reserved for session, onboarding, and device-scoped flags.
7. [finished] The repo has active test coverage in both layers: backend has Jest unit/e2e scaffolding, and frontend has multiple widget/screen tests under frontend/test.
8. [finished] SCREENS.md and some docs appear to reflect an older source tree or source-of-truth path, so documentation should be reconciled with the current repository layout before relying on it as the only inventory.

metadata.summary: Audited the WAH4P repository and saved a clear project snapshot for future work. The repo is split into a NestJS backend, a Flutter frontend, and supporting SQL/docs. The backend is a secured API with global API-key protection, throttling, strict env validation, Supabase auth/data integration, and Resend email delivery. The frontend is a Flutter mobile app with centralized theming, secure local storage, a startup gate, and MPIN/session lifecycle handling. The docs describe a Supabase/Postgres source of truth for patient data, with frontend screens mostly acting as presentation and interaction layers. I also captured the current testing surface and a couple of documentation mismatches that future work should reconcile.
